question: Define an exterior point of a subset A in a metric space (X, d).
expectedPoints: An exterior point is a point x in X for which there exists an open ball centered at x that is entirely contained in the complement of A.
More formally, x is an exterior point of A if there exists some ε > 0 such that B(x, ε) ⊆ A^c.
The set of all exterior points of A is called the exterior of A and is an open set.

question: Prove that a point x is a boundary point of a set A in a metric space if and only if every open ball centered at x intersects both A and its complement.
expectedPoints: (⇒) If x is a boundary point (x ∈ ∂A), then by definition, every neighborhood of x intersects both A and A^c. Since every open ball B(x, ε) is a neighborhood, the condition holds.
(⇐) Conversely, if every B(x, ε) intersects both A and A^c, then every neighborhood of x (which contains some open ball) must also intersect both sets. This is the definition of a boundary point.
This characterization is often used as the working definition for the boundary ∂A.

question: Let A be a subset of a metric space (X, d). Prove that the closure of A is equal to the union of the interior of A and the boundary of A. Furthermore, show that these three sets (int(A), ∂A, ext(A)) form a partition of the entire space X.
expectedPoints: Proof of cl(A) = int(A) ∪ ∂A: Any point in cl(A) is either an interior point (if it has a neighborhood contained in A) or a limit point (where every neighborhood intersects A). A limit point that is not interior must have every neighborhood also intersect A^c, making it a boundary point. Conversely, any point in int(A) or ∂A is clearly in the closure.

Proof of partition: Show int(A), ∂A, and ext(A) are pairwise disjoint. A point cannot be simultaneously interior (neighborhood in A) and exterior (neighborhood in A^c). A boundary point, by definition, is neither interior nor exterior.

Proof that the union is X: For any point x in X, every open ball around x is either entirely in A (making x interior), entirely in A^c (making x exterior), or intersects both (making x a boundary point). These are the only three possibilities, so the union covers X.
